title: Everything is Recorded Now | Andreessen Horowitz
gist: a16z's David Haber argues default meeting recording is an irreversible, already-happened shift because AI agents that ingest a company's full voice context become dramatically better assistants. He predicts a new voice-organized enterprise system-of-record category, a flip in the recording default within ~6 months, and a structural advantage for AI-native and verbal-culture companies over incumbents.

insights.claim: David Haber argues that default recording of work conversations has already happened without debate and is not a reversible trend, because the bottom-up productivity gains for individuals and top-down oversight gains for leaders are too large to undo.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Treat pervasive meeting-recording infrastructure as a structural inevitability, not a niche tool; the addressable market is the whole enterprise.
insights.quote: you should probably assume that everything you say at work is getting recorded for here on out.
insights.locator: One of the biggest ways that AI

insights.claim: Haber predicts the enterprise default will flip within roughly six months from 'don't record unless you opt in' to 'assume you're being recorded unless a meeting is explicitly designated otherwise.'
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: A near-term inflection point to time investments in recording/voice tooling and to watch for in corporate policy changes.
insights.quote: I'd bet this is far less contested six months from now than it is today.
insights.locator: This is where the inevitability comes from

insights.claim: Haber frames meeting recording as merely the conversational extension of the principle professionals already apply to text — never put anything in writing you wouldn't want made public — since screenshots get forwarded, emails get subpoenaed, and Slack messages end up in discovery.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Reframes the privacy objection as already-conceded, undercutting resistance and supporting the inevitability thesis.
insights.quote: Meeting recording is the same principle, applied to conversation.
insights.locator: The deeper reason is that the old principle

insights.claim: Haber's key framework is that AI must be onboarded like a new employee — through attending meetings and learning by osmosis — rather than by ingesting CRM systems or wikis, because meetings are where culture, expectations, and edge-case handling actually live.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Implies the most valuable AI-context products will plug into live conversation, not static document stores.
insights.quote: You invite them to meetings and let them learn through osmosis. This is because meetings are where culture resides, where expectations live, where edge case handling gets done.
insights.locator: The key insight is that you need to onboard AI

insights.claim: Haber claims an AI model that has ingested two years of a company's internal discussion is simply a better assistant than one that only read the documentation, citing OpenAI running with essentially everything recorded and agents standing in for senior leaders in meetings they can't attend.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Ingested conversational context is the differentiating moat for enterprise AI assistants; data access, not model quality alone, decides assistant value.
insights.quote: The model that's ingested two years of your company's internal discussion is simply a better assistant than the one that only read your documentation.
insights.locator: Bridgewater was ahead of its time here

insights.claim: Haber cites Granola as the clearest example of a recording-based tool that has better context on a16z's culture, investments, and thinking than almost any other tool the firm uses, specifically because it has 'been in the room.'
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: A live proof point that 'in-the-room' presence beats document access; a16z is signaling conviction in voice-context tooling it likely backs.
insights.quote: it has better context on a16z's culture, our investments, and how we actually think than almost any other tool we use, because it's been in the room.
insights.locator: Bridgewater was ahead of its time here

themes.theme: Voice as the new system of record

insights.claim: Haber predicts a new category of enterprise software organized around voice instead of text, because the highest-value context lives in conversation — the nuance on a customer call, the real argument in a product review, the offhand comment that changes the roadmap — rather than in structured CRM, ticket, and doc data.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Points to a greenfield software category; the open question of who owns the voice system-of-record layer is the investable prize.
insights.quote: What's emerging is a new category of enterprise software, organized around voice instead of text.
insights.locator: What's emerging is a new category

insights.claim: Haber argues LLMs are uniquely good at converting unstructured voice data into structured, searchable, queryable form, making this a large enterprise opportunity that is still early in terms of what the software layer looks like and who owns it.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: The ownership of the voice-to-structured-data layer is undecided and early — a window for startups before an incumbent locks it in.
insights.quote: That's a large enterprise opportunity, and we're still early in understanding what the software layer looks like and who owns it.

insights.claim: Haber identifies two advantages forgone by not recording: a bottom-up force-multiplier for individual contributors who can see how to improve the company, and an under-discussed top-down oversight advantage for execs who need a handle on what's getting built.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Recording sells on both productivity and governance — broadening the buyer set beyond ICs to execs and boards.
insights.quote: There are two advantages you give up by not recording.

insights.claim: Haber argues AI does not solve the internal alignment problem and that un-shipping something is far more expensive than shipping it, so execs need their AI present in meetings they can't attend to flag what matters.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: As AI makes ICs 10x more productive, oversight tooling becomes a necessity rather than optional — a distinct demand vector for recording.
insights.quote: AI might make your best ICs 10x more productive, but it doesn't solve the alignment problem inside a company, and un-shipping something is far more expensive than shipping it.

insights.claim: Haber claims both the bottom-up and top-down advantages compound, with every recorded meeting making the system smarter.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Compounding context implies a data-network-effect moat and a first-mover advantage for early recorders.
insights.quote: Both advantages compound. Every recorded meeting makes the system smarter.

insights.claim: Haber argues companies cluster into verbal cultures (Shopify, OpenAI) and written cultures (Stripe, Anthropic), and that verbal cultures gain a compounding advantage in an AI-native world because AI can finally capture and synthesize the conversational context that previously evaporated.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: AI disproportionately benefits verbal-culture firms; their historical context bottleneck dissolves, shifting relative competitive position.
insights.quote: Verbal cultures, the Shopifys and OpenAIs, have a compounding advantage in an AI-native world, where written cultures like Stripe or Anthropic already capture most of their context by construction.

insights.claim: Haber asserts AI will promote and enhance verbal culture disproportionately, while still benefiting written-culture companies that give AI access to their thoughtful writing.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: A lens for assessing which companies extract the most leverage from AI based on how their context is encoded.
insights.quote: overall, I think AI is going to promote and enhance verbal culture disproportionately.

insights.claim: Haber argues recording is a decisive wedge between smaller AI-native companies, for whom it's the obvious default, and incumbents that must overcome the inertia and legal fear of not doing it — where big-company executives respond with 'have you ever been sued before?'
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Recording adoption is a differentiator favoring startups; legal-risk inertia at incumbents is a measurable competitive handicap.
insights.quote: That makes recording a decisive wedge between smaller, AI-native companies, for whom it's the obvious default, and incumbents that have to overcome the inertia of not doing it.
insights.locator: That makes recording a decisive wedge

insights.claim: Haber predicts companies will land on special designations for sensitive meetings — something like an 'AC Priv' don't-record tag for HR and legal — but that these controls will be gameable and retrofitted on top of widespread recording that simply happens because it's too hard to stop.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Governance and access-control products will be a follow-on layer; recording arrives first, controls second.
insights.quote: My bet is that widespread recording simply happens, because it's too hard to stop, and the controls get retrofitted on top.
insights.locator: We'll probably land on special designations

themes.theme: Bridgewater precedent and governance call to action

insights.claim: Haber cites Bridgewater as ahead of its time and prescient for making record-everything an institutional policy that looked eccentric for years.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: An existing institutional proof point that pervasive recording can be a durable, deliberate strategy rather than a fad.
insights.quote: Recording everything as institutional policy looked eccentric for years, and it turns out it was prescient.
insights.locator: Bridgewater was ahead of its time here

insights.claim: Haber positions the living context layer as being built inside companies right now whether they pay attention or not, framing the strategic question not as whether recording happens but whether a company gets there first and builds the right governance while it still has the advantage of choosing.
insights.speaker: David Haber
insights.so_what: Boards and operators face a now-or-never governance decision; first-mover firms can shape controls on their own terms.
insights.quote: The question isn't whether this happens. It's whether you get there first, and build the right governance around it while you still have the advantage of choosing.
insights.locator: It's a great time to be an operator
